context.description: A philosophical discussion defines “wrong” as an action that cannot be justified coherently within oneself, even when it violates law or morality. It then uses a politician’s self-image to show how moral certainty can justify extreme actions against perceived enemies. The argument turns to self-deception and the circular nature of self-perception.
normalization_notes: Source text is German; summary and tags are translated into English.
Title inferred from source file name.

rows.paragraph_text: “Wrong” is defined as an act that cannot be justified coherently within oneself, even if it is illegal or morally condemned. A politician thought experiment shows how a self-image of goodness can support extreme measures against perceived enemies. The passage argues that self-deception and circular self-perception prevent recognition of one’s own failure.

content.free_text: book_page book_body_content theoretical_discussion Book page on self-justification, morality, self-image, and self-deception. Philosophical discussion uses a politician thought experiment to examine how perceived righteousness can rationalize extreme actions.
